findings.body:
**Verdict: MINOR ISSUES**

1. **§3.4 Receipt-before-inject chooses loss over duplicate, but recovery is overstated.**  
   The spec says the direct-inject receipt-then-crash window is “visible and recoverable,” yet the crash table says boot marks the row `delivered` and performs no inject. That is not recoverable without a user-visible loss report or reconciliation path. Resolution: either emit an explicit “possibly not injected” attention item for this crash state, or rename it as an accepted silent-loss window and justify why it is acceptable.

2. **§1 / §3.4 SQLite durability assumptions need sharper operational boundaries.**  
   `synchronous=FULL` + WAL improves local durability but does not guarantee survival across all filesystem, disk-cache, network-volume, or OS settings. Since this spec leans heavily on “local durable custody,” it should state supported storage assumptions: local disk only, no network-mounted state dir, expected SQLite fsync semantics, and what happens on WAL/SHM partial corruption.

3. **§2.2 Fall-through on custody refusal can violate the stated routing decision.**  
   Refusal falls through to today’s local dispatch, even when the router verdict was “queued” or “placement-blocked.” The spec acknowledges ordering violations, but not wrong-place delivery under pressure, which is one of the original harms. Resolution: distinguish refusal causes. For overload/corrupt store, consider fail-closed user notice or explicit degraded delivery mode rather than automatically injecting locally.

4. **§3.2 / §4.3 Held-row state is split between DB and memory.**  
   `first_held_at` appears in the DB schema, but §3.2 says per-head `first_held_at` lives in the in-memory held-set to preserve no-row-I/O. That creates ambiguity after restart or drain reconstruction. Resolution: make the DB column authoritative, with memory as cache only, or remove the DB column and explicitly accept hold-budget reset on restart.

5. **§5.1 Survivor “loss-suspected” respawn risks duplicate acting.**  
   Top-K synthetic re-placement after a stale heartbeat can re-run work if the dead machine delivered after its last heartbeat. The spec names this duplicate window, but the mitigation is mostly copy and recency filtering. For an “acting agent,” this is the same duplicate-acting class §3.4 treats as worse than loss. Resolution: explain why survivor respawn is acceptable despite that priority, or gate it behind operator confirmation for high-risk topics.

6. **Language clarity: too much convergence-history in normative spec.**  
   Many sections include “round-2/round-3 reviewer” archaeology. It helps reviewers but obscures the final design. Resolution: move review-history rationale to an appendix and keep the main spec normative: requirements, invariants, failure modes, tests.

Note: supporting context was truncated, so findings about fit with the broader session-pool architecture are based only on the visible excerpt.
